one_line_takeaway: MulVul's coarse-to-fine Router-Detector multi-agent architecture combined with cross-model (Claude-generates/GPT-4o-executes) prompt evolution and SCALE-based contrastive retrieval raises fine-grained (130-CWE-type) vulnerability detection on PrimeVul from a 3.86% Macro-F1 GPT-4o baseline to 34.79% Macro-F1, with ablations showing retrieval grounding (-12.76 F1 if removed) and cross-model prompt evolution (-11.76 F1 if removed) as the two largest contributors, and with the largest relative gains concentrated on few-shot CWE types.
key_finding: At category level (10 coarse categories), MulVul reaches 50.41% Macro-F1 (Macro-Precision 44.31%, Macro-Recall 58.45%), beating the strongest baseline LLMVulExp (41.50% Macro-F1) by 8.91 points; LLM×CPG has higher recall (62.81%) but much lower precision (27.44%), i.e. over-prediction. At the harder fine-grained type level (130 CWE types), MulVul achieves 34.79% Macro-F1 (27.90% Macro-Precision, 46.19% Macro-Recall), a 41.5% relative improvement over the best baseline LLM×CPG (24.58% Macro-F1); GPT-4o alone scores only 3.86% Macro-F1 at type level. The routing parameter k trades recall for precision (Recall rises from 29.10% at k=1 to 48.37% at k=5; Precision falls from 39.58% to 26.54%), with Macro-F1 peaking at k=3 (34.79%) and staying roughly flat for k=4,5. Ablation (Table 4, type-level Macro-F1 for the reported full-model run of 34.56%): removing retrieval is the single largest driver, dropping F1 to 21.80% (-12.76); removing cross-model evolution (replacing with manual prompts) drops F1 to 22.80% (-11.76, i.e. the paper's headline '51.6% boost' figure = (34.56-22.80)/22.80); removing the multi-agent architecture (single unified prompt/model instead of Router+Detectors) drops F1 to 28.61% (-5.95); the no-RAG/no-agents/no-evolution GPT-4o baseline scores 3.86% (-30.70). On few-shot CWE types (<500 samples), MulVul reaches ~48% F1 for CWEs with <100 samples versus ~25% for the best baseline (LLMVulExp), and in the appendix's aggregate few-shot metric (<500 samples) MulVul achieves 0.228 Tail F1 vs. 0.095 (LLM×CPG), 0.036 (LLMVulExp), and 0.094 (VISION) -- roughly 2.4x and 6.3x the two named comparisons -- with the lowest Gini coefficient (0.396) indicating the most uniform performance across CWE frequency tiers. Even for CWE types with >500 samples MulVul retains a 12+ point F1 lead over baselines.
core_contribution: MulVul, a retrieval-augmented multi-agent LLM framework for multi-class code vulnerability (CWE) detection. It has two novel pieces the paper credits as its own contributions: (1) a coarse-to-fine Router-Detector agent architecture aligned to the two-level CWE hierarchy, where a Router agent predicts top-k coarse categories using a global retrieval tool over a SCALE-encoded knowledge base, and only the corresponding category-specific Detector agents (each using a contrastive retrieval tool over in-category positives, clean negatives, and out-of-category hard negatives) are invoked to identify fine-grained CWE types, avoiding the cost of invoking every possible specialized agent and avoiding inter-agent error cascades by having Detectors operate in isolation; and (2) Cross-Model Prompt Evolution, an automatic prompt-optimization mechanism that decouples prompt generation from prompt evaluation across two different frozen LLMs -- a generator/evolution model M_evo (Claude Opus 4.5 in their implementation) that proposes and mutates candidate prompts via an EVOLVE procedure (elitism + rank-based selection + LLM-driven mutation), and a separate executor model M_exec (GPT-4o) that runs the Router/Detector agents on training data and returns fitness feedback (Recall@k for the Router, F1 for Detectors), with final prompt selection done on a held-out validation set. The stated motivation for the cross-model split is to avoid the 'self-correction bias' of single-backbone prompt optimizers (e.g., APE, EvoPrompt, OPRO, cited as prior work, not as MulVul's own methods).
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the security-red-team sense; this is a defensive detection task. The setting is: given a code snippet x, output a set of predicted CWE vulnerability labels (or 'non-vulnerable'), where the code may contain zero, one, or multiple real (non-adversarially-crafted) vulnerabilities. The 'threats' addressed are failure modes of the LLM-based detector itself, not of the code under analysis: (i) heterogeneity of vulnerability patterns overwhelming a single unified model, (ii) unscalable manual prompt engineering across ~130 CWE types, and (iii) hallucination/error-cascade risk in multi-agent pipelines where retrieval grounding and detector isolation are the proposed mitigations. The paper's own Limitations section separately flags a dual-use/societal risk: the framework could in principle be repurposed by malicious actors to hunt for zero-day vulnerabilities, and automation bias could cause developers to under-scrutinize code given inevitable false negatives -- but this is explicitly an ethical caveat, not the paper's evaluated threat model.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Limitations section, verbatim scope): (1) evaluated exclusively on PrimeVul, C/C++ code only -- generalization to other languages (Java, Python) and other benchmarks is untested; (2) inference cost is 1+k LLM API calls per sample plus a separately expensive offline iterative prompt-evolution phase, which may be impractical for resource-constrained or large-scale batch settings; (3) the cross-model evolution's transferability claim is only lightly tested -- experiments primarily use GPT-4o as the execution model, so whether evolved prompts generalize to other executor LLMs was not thoroughly evaluated; (4) dual-use/misuse risk -- the same detection capability could be used by attackers to discover zero-day vulnerabilities for exploitation, and automation bias could cause developers to reduce manual code scrutiny despite the system's inevitable false negatives. Beyond the authors' own list: absolute Macro-F1 at the fine-grained CWE level remains low in absolute terms (34.79%), meaning the majority of individual vulnerability types are still poorly detected even by the best system; the knowledge base K is built from labeled training data and there is no discussion of how retrieval degrades if the target vulnerability type has zero or near-zero training examples in K (beyond the <10-sample 'Ext. Rare' tier of 7 CWE types covering only 32 total samples, which is a very thin evaluation slice); and the SCALE structured-comment generation step and UniXcoder embeddings are both reused prior components (Wen et al. 2024; Guo et al. 2022) rather than novel contributions, so part of MulVul's grounding quality is inherited rather than newly validated.

datasets_used: PrimeVul (Ding et al., 2024): 6,968 vulnerable and 229,764 benign C/C++ functions across 10 coarse categories and 130 CWE types
quantitative_results: Routing parameter k sweep (type-level Macro-F1): k=1: 33.51%, k=2: 34.56%, k=3: 34.79% (peak), k=4: 34.34%, k=5: 34.27%
Ablation (type-level Macro-F1, full model reported here as 34.56%): GPT-4o only 3.86% (Δ=-30.70); w/o Retrieval 21.80% (Δ=-12.76); w/o Agents 28.61% (Δ=-5.95); w/o Evolution 22.80% (Δ=-11.76)
Few-shot (<100 samples) F1: MulVul ~48% vs. best baseline LLMVulExp ~25%

Few-shot aggregate metrics (<500 samples, Table 6): Tail F1 -- MulVul 0.228, LLM×CPG 0.095, LLMVulExp 0.036, VISION 0.094; Fail Threshold (lower better) -- MulVul 51, LLM×CPG 153, LLMVulExp 311, VISION 51; Tail Coverage -- MulVul 65.6%, LLM×CPG 40.9%, LLMVulExp 11.8%, VISION 55.9%; Gini Coefficient (lower better) -- MulVul 0.396, LLM×CPG 0.580, LLMVulExp 0.695, VISION 0.495

PrimeVul class-imbalance breakdown (Table 5): Head tier (≥5k samples) = 12 CWEs / 140,080 samples / 69.6% share; down to Ext. Rare tier (<10 samples) = 7 CWEs / 32 samples / <0.1% share, out of 130 CWE types / 201,166 total samples
baselines_compared: GPT-4o (zero-shot prompting, no fine-tuning or demonstrations)
LLM×CPG (Lekssays et al. 2025) -- LoRA fine-tuned Qwen2.5-32B with code-property-graph-guided context
LLMVulExp (Mao et al. 2025) -- LoRA fine-tuned CodeLlama-7B with chain-of-thought explanations
VISION (Egea et al. 2025) -- Devign GNN with counterfactual augmentation
